title: Reverse power rule: sums & multiples
description: Find the indefinite integrals of polynomials.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and indefinite integrals: basic rules and notation: reverse power rule

hints:
We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (6x^2-5x+3)\,dx=6\int x^2\,dx-5\int x\,dx+3\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (6x^2-5x+3)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle6\int x^2\,dx-5\int x\,dx+3\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=6\dfrac{x^3}{3}-5\dfrac{x^2}{2}+3\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=2x^3-\dfrac52x^2+3x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (6x^2-5x+3)\,dx=2x^3-\dfrac52x^2+3x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (-3 x^4 -6 x^2 +8)\,dx= -3\int x^4\,dx -6\int x^2\,dx +8\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (-3 x^4 -6 x^2 +8)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -3\int x^4\,dx -6\int x^2\,dx +8\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=-3 \dfrac{x^5}{5} -6\dfrac{x^3}{3} +8\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{5} x^5 -2 x^3 +8 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (-3 x^4 -6 x^2 +8)\,dx=-\dfrac{3}{5} x^5 -2 x^3 +8 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (2 x^3 +2 x -3)\,dx= 2\int x^3\,dx +2\int x\,dx -3\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (2 x^3 +2 x -3)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 2\int x^3\,dx +2\int x\,dx -3\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=2 \dfrac{x^4}{4} +2\dfrac{x^2}{2} -3\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} x^4 +x^2 -3 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (2 x^3 +2 x -3)\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2} x^4 +x^2 -3 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (7 x^6 +7 x^3 +4)\,dx= 7\int x^6\,dx +7\int x^3\,dx +4\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (7 x^6 +7 x^3 +4)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 7\int x^6\,dx +7\int x^3\,dx +4\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=7 \dfrac{x^7}{7} +7\dfrac{x^4}{4} +4\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=x^7 +\dfrac{7}{4} x^4 +4 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (7 x^6 +7 x^3 +4)\,dx=x^7 +\dfrac{7}{4} x^4 +4 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (5 x^4 +3 x^3 -2)\,dx= 5\int x^4\,dx +3\int x^3\,dx -2\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (5 x^4 +3 x^3 -2)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 5\int x^4\,dx +3\int x^3\,dx -2\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=5 \dfrac{x^5}{5} +3\dfrac{x^4}{4} -2\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=x^5 +\dfrac{3}{4} x^4 -2 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (5 x^4 +3 x^3 -2)\,dx=x^5 +\dfrac{3}{4} x^4 -2 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int ( x^4 + x +9)\,dx= \int x^4\,dx +\int x\,dx +9\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int ( x^4 + x +9)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int x^4\,dx +\int x\,dx +9\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&= \dfrac{x^5}{5} +\dfrac{x^2}{2} +9\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{5} x^5 +\dfrac{1}{2} x^2 +9 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int ( x^4 + x +9)\,dx=\dfrac{1}{5} x^5 +\dfrac{1}{2} x^2 +9 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (-4 x^3 -8 x^2 -4)\,dx= -4\int x^3\,dx -8\int x^2\,dx -4\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (-4 x^3 -8 x^2 -4)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -4\int x^3\,dx -8\int x^2\,dx -4\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=-4 \dfrac{x^4}{4} -8\dfrac{x^3}{3} -4\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=- x^4 -\dfrac{8}{3} x^3 -4 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (-4 x^3 -8 x^2 -4)\,dx=- x^4 -\dfrac{8}{3} x^3 -4 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (9 x^5 -3 x^2 +7)\,dx= 9\int x^5\,dx -3\int x^2\,dx +7\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (9 x^5 -3 x^2 +7)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 9\int x^5\,dx -3\int x^2\,dx +7\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=9 \dfrac{x^6}{6} -3\dfrac{x^3}{3} +7\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{2} x^6 - x^3 +7 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (9 x^5 -3 x^2 +7)\,dx=\dfrac{3}{2} x^6 - x^3 +7 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (-2 x^4 -4 x^2 -5)\,dx= -2\int x^4\,dx -4\int x^2\,dx -5\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (-2 x^4 -4 x^2 -5)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -2\int x^4\,dx -4\int x^2\,dx -5\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=-2 \dfrac{x^5}{5} -4\dfrac{x^3}{3} -5\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{2}{5} x^5 -\dfrac{4}{3} x^3 -5 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (-2 x^4 -4 x^2 -5)\,dx=-\dfrac{2}{5} x^5 -\dfrac{4}{3} x^3 -5 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (-9 x^2 +9 x +2)\,dx= -9\int x^2\,dx +9\int x\,dx +2\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (-9 x^2 +9 x +2)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -9\int x^2\,dx +9\int x\,dx +2\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=-9 \dfrac{x^3}{3} +9\dfrac{x^2}{2} +2\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=-3 x^3 +\dfrac{9}{2} x^2 +2 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (-9 x^2 +9 x +2)\,dx=-3 x^3 +\dfrac{9}{2} x^2 +2 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (- x^3 -8 x +5)\,dx= -1\int x^3\,dx -8\int x\,dx +5\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (- x^3 -8 x +5)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -\int x^3\,dx -8\int x\,dx +5\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=- \dfrac{x^4}{4} -8\dfrac{x^2}{2} +5\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{4} x^4 -4 x^2 +5 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (- x^3 -8 x +5)\,dx=-\dfrac{1}{4} x^4 -4 x^2 +5 x+C$

We can use the sum rule and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int [f(x)+g(x)]dx=\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx+\int g(x)\,dx
\\\\\\
&\displaystyle\int k\cdot f(x)=\displaystyle k\cdot\int f(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

Using the sum and the constant multiple rules, we can rewrite our integral as follows:

$\displaystyle\int (3 x^5 - x^3 +6)\,dx= 3\int x^5\,dx -\int x^3\,dx +6\int 1\,dx$

Now we can find each indefinite integral using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

*Note: we can only use the reverse power rule because $n \neq -1$.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (3 x^5 - x^3 +6)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 3\int x^5\,dx -\int x^3\,dx +6\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=3 \dfrac{x^6}{6} -\dfrac{x^4}{4} +6\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} x^6 -\dfrac{1}{4} x^4 +6 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (3 x^5 - x^3 +6)\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2} x^6 -\dfrac{1}{4} x^4 +6 x+C$
